news_summary: Efforts to restore forests, advance renewable energy stability, enhance marine ecosystems, and apply innovative scientific techniques highlight today's positive environmental progress.
news_summary_fr: Les efforts visant à restaurer les forêts, à renforcer la fiabilité des énergies renouvelables, à préserver les écosystèmes marins et à mettre en œuvre des techniques scientifiques innovantes témoignent des progrès environnementaux réalisés aujourd'hui.
news_summary_es: Las iniciativas para restaurar los bosques, fomentar la estabilidad de las energías renovables, mejorar los ecosistemas marinos y aplicar técnicas científicas innovadoras ponen de relieve los avances medioambientales positivos que se están logrando en la actualidad.

articles.title: How New Mexico is ‘building a forest’ by solving a seedling shortage
articles.content:
This story was originally published by High Country News and is reproduced here as part of the Climate Desk collaboration.

Recovery from the Hermit’s Peak-Calf Canyon Fire has been daunting. Residents are still waiting for disaster relief payments even as floods sweep through the ashy burn scar, contaminating the drinking water downstream. And then there’s the forest itself: in desperate need of new trees but lacking the necessary seedlings.

Wildfires have burned 7 million acres across New Mexico since 2000, and millions of seedlings are needed to replant the burned areas. The Hermit’s Peak-Calf Canyon Fire alone, the largest in state history, needs 17.6 million seedlings. Trees play an integral role in restabilizing burned hillsides and protecting the drinking water sources below them. But current reforestation facilities lack the capacity to keep up with demand, creating a dire shortage. Experts estimate it would take 50 years to replant the Hermit’s Peak-Calf Canyon burn scar at current rates.

That’s where the New Mexico Reforestation Center comes in. Conceived in 2022 as a collaboration between the state Forestry Division, University of New Mexico, New Mexico State University, and New Mexico Highlands University, the center is now about to break ground on an “absolutely massive” greenhouse facility that expands existing infrastructure in the northwestern part of the state, Director Jennifer Auchter told High Country News.

The greenhouses, which will eventually total 155,000 square feet in size, are an essential part of the seed-to-seedling-to-tree post-fire reforestation pipeline, which involves processing over 1,500 pounds of native seed for future planting while researching ways to help seedlings survive an even hotter and drier future. (The enterprise has a uniquely New Mexican flavor: Auchter said that a repurposed chili roaster is used to extract seeds from cones and pods at the existing seed-processing facility.)

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

High Country News caught up with Auchter in mid-April as she prepared for the greenhouse groundbreaking to talk about how the center will more than triple the state’s current seedling production capacity and why a “right tree, right place” approach is important for the success of modern reforestation.

This conversation has been edited for length and clarity.

Q. Why is re-planting a forest after a wildfire so important?

A. This is my favorite part of the whole story. My background is in earth science, watershed management, fluvial systems, and that sort of thing. So I think about this from a “forest being water infrastructure” perspective, particularly in New Mexico and the Southwest. Here, it’s the snowpack in the winter that feeds our rivers and streams. People have tried to quantify this a bunch of different ways, but it’s something like 70 percent of all the water we use is coming from a forest, whether it’s the snowpack or the precipitation that streams capture.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The likelihood of a forest actually regenerating (after a high-severity wildfire) is not very likely in our lifetimes. It could be decades to even centuries. For us, because of our water infrastructure here, we really need to conserve forests even just to have a water source.

Courtesy of Jennifer Auchter

Q. What are New Mexico’s current reforestation needs, given several large wildfires in recent years?

A. The state’s current burn scars need 385 million trees, and that doesn’t include future wildfires. That’s just the existing backlog.

Q. How many seedlings can current operations in New Mexico grow right now? Tell us about the gap the New Mexico Reforestation Center is seeking to fill.

A. We say 300,000, but we don’t even usually hit that mark. It’s usually more like 250,000 seedlings per year, and those are grown at the John T. Harrington Forestry Research Center, which is a pretty small and pretty old nursery.

Most of our tree seedlings are purchased from growers in Idaho. That’s not terrible, but they’re not exactly from the right climate, they’re not exactly from the right elevation, and then when they travel over land, they don’t do as well in planting. They’re kind of shocked when they get here.

Q. What kind of research has been going on to help set the New Mexico Reforestation Center and its replanting efforts up for success?

A. What the University of New Mexico is responsible for is doing a lot of the research on modeling: predicting seedling survival based on the site and based on the projected climate. So, when we’re selecting sites and planting out trees, we’re actually doing this for the 2100 climate, not for today’s climate. We want to make sure that these trees are surviving long-term. Then, once they plant it out, they will also do monitoring with drones, and on-the-ground monitoring as well.

Q. How else are researchers trying to ensure seedlings can survive in hotter, drier conditions?

A. New Mexico State University researchers are doing drought conditioning, so putting the seedlings under a lower amount of irrigation. They’re drought-stressing ponderosa pine and some of the other species as seedlings, so that when they plant them out, they are ready for that dry environment. There’s another thing that they’re doing specifically for aspen seedling survival in a post-fire environment: planting the seedling next to a log, for example, and trying to give it just a bit of shade while it’s in those early establishing months and years. They’re seeing higher survival for the seedlings that are planted in the shade areas.

There’s a lot of research going on to try to optimize this, bigger-scale. Once we jump from 300,000 seedlings to 5 million seedlings, ideally, by that time, we will have sorted out the very best methods that are the most successful across the whole reforestation pipeline, from seeds to seedling to planting.

Ponderosa pine cones are cut to extract seeds. Courtesy of New Mexico Reforestation Center / Josh Sloan

Q. Did you look to any other states for reforestation inspiration? What did you learn?

A. The Pacific Northwest is really great at reforesting. At least in the early days, I think there was an idea that we would model reforestation in the Southwest after reforestation in the Pacific Northwest. But we really just need to have our own regionally tailored methods, regionally appropriate stock — the genetics of the seedling — all of that is really important.

Q. How will the new forestry center benefit the broader region?

A. We do anticipate that, particularly in other areas of the Four Corners states, our seedlings will be appropriate. For example, northern Arizona, Flagstaff — that’s a similar elevation, similar species. That could be said for some areas of Colorado as well. So, I think we probably will focus on New Mexico first, but we do expect to actually be able to provide more seedlings longer term, once we start reforesting our own burn scars.

There’s not an official sort of hub or resource collaboration for the Southwest. There are a lot of groups working here — NGOs, government agencies, universities — there’s a lot of people doing a lot of different things, but they’re generally pretty disjointed. We envision the NMRC bringing those people together.

Q. What’s an important part of the reforestation process that’s sometimes overlooked?

A. I think it’s important to highlight the whole reforestation pipeline. The seed collection work that New Mexico Highlands University does is sort of this hidden part of it, right? The greenhouses and the seedlings are flashy, and it’s easy to take pictures and cover that, but the seed collection work is tedious, time-consuming, and requires a massive labor development approach. It’s rugged, it’s hard.

Q. What do you wish more people knew about reforestation?

A. I’ve heard a couple of different times, “Wow, that’s an expensive greenhouse.” It’s really not just a greenhouse. There’s so much more that goes into building a forest. It’s quite a daunting action, bringing that to the forefront in any way we can, raising awareness of how many bodies have touched the seed or the seedling or planted it out from the time that seed is collected to when it’s actually growing.

Q. What kind of long-lasting impact can reforestation have on a community post-fire, and how are you going about that in New Mexico?

A. I’ll use one example that we just finished last week. We had 48 students across grades from a charter school near Mora come to the seed collection facility, and we had the kids, who lived through wildfire, create a piece of art that was something special to them. During this whole workshop, while they were creating this art, we had some of our forestry experts talk about just the process of reforestation and what all goes into it. The point of it is really to get kids engaged locally at a younger age, so that as they go through school next year, those same kids will come back and get to see maybe some of the seeds they worked with are now in pots as seedlings, and maybe in three years, they get to go see that tree planted out in the forest. Keeping them engaged along the whole timeline of reforestation, to sort of imprint upon them how important conservation is. Because once it’s gone, it takes a long time to bring it back.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant and tangible environmental initiative addressing the large-scale reforestation needs in New Mexico following devastating wildfires. The creation of the New Mexico Reforestation Center and its massive greenhouse facility will more than triple seedling production capacity, which is critical for restoring forests that stabilize watersheds and protect drinking water. The story includes detailed context on the scale of the problem, innovative research for climate-adapted seedlings, and community engagement, demonstrating broad positive impact and substance.
articles.personality_title: New Mexico launches major effort to grow millions of trees after wildfires
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – New Mexico has faced large wildfires in recent years, burning millions of acres of forest. These forests are important because they help protect water supplies and prevent soil from washing away. After a fire, new trees are needed to help the land recover, but there are not enough seedlings to plant.

**What happened** – To solve this problem, New Mexico created the New Mexico Reforestation Center in 2022. This center is building a huge greenhouse that will be 155,000 square feet in size. It will increase the number of tree seedlings grown in the state from about 250,000 per year to 5 million. The center is a team effort involving state forestry officials and three universities. They also research ways to grow trees that can survive hotter and drier weather.

**Impact** – This new center will help replant millions of trees faster than before, which is vital because forests support water supplies and stop floods and erosion. Currently, it could take 50 years to replant just one major burned area at the current pace. By growing local seedlings adapted to New Mexico’s climate, the trees will have a better chance to survive and thrive. The project also brings together different groups in the Southwest to work on reforestation.

**What's next step** – The greenhouse construction is about to start, and the center will begin producing many more seedlings. Researchers will continue testing how to grow trees that handle drought and heat better. The center plans to involve local communities and schools to teach about reforestation, helping people understand and support the long-term work.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New Mexico’s new Reforestation Center will grow millions of climate-ready tree seedlings to restore forests faster and protect water sources after wildfires.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Nouveau-Mexique lance un grand projet pour cultiver des millions d’arbres après les incendies
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Nouveau-Mexique a connu de grands incendies ces dernières années, brûlant des millions d’acres de forêt. Ces forêts sont importantes car elles protègent les sources d’eau et empêchent l’érosion des sols. Après un incendie, il faut planter de nouveaux arbres, mais il manque de plants pour cela.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Pour résoudre ce problème, le Nouveau-Mexique a créé en 2022 le Centre de Reboisement du Nouveau-Mexique. Ce centre construit une serre immense de 14 400 mètres carrés. Elle permettra de multiplier par vingt la production annuelle de plants d’arbres, passant d’environ 250 000 à 5 millions. Ce projet réunit des responsables forestiers et trois universités. Ils étudient aussi comment faire pousser des arbres capables de résister à un climat plus chaud et sec.

**Impact** – Ce nouveau centre aidera à replanter des millions d’arbres beaucoup plus vite, ce qui est essentiel car les forêts soutiennent les ressources en eau et évitent les inondations et l’érosion. Aujourd’hui, il faudrait 50 ans pour replanter une seule grande zone brûlée. En cultivant des plants adaptés au climat local, les arbres auront plus de chances de survivre. Ce projet rassemble aussi différents acteurs du Sud-Ouest pour travailler ensemble au reboisement.

**Prochaine étape** – La construction de la serre va bientôt commencer, et le centre commencera à produire beaucoup plus de plants. Les chercheurs continueront à tester comment faire pousser des arbres résistants à la sécheresse et à la chaleur. Le centre prévoit d’impliquer les communautés locales et les écoles pour sensibiliser au reboisement et soutenir ce travail à long terme.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le nouveau Centre de Reboisement du Nouveau-Mexique produira des millions de plants d’arbres adaptés au climat pour restaurer plus vite les forêts et protéger l’eau après les incendies.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo México inicia gran proyecto para cultivar millones de árboles tras incendios
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Nuevo México ha sufrido grandes incendios en los últimos años, quemando millones de acres de bosque. Estos bosques son importantes porque ayudan a proteger el agua y evitar que la tierra se erosione. Después de un incendio, se necesitan nuevos árboles, pero faltan plantas para sembrar.

**Qué pasó** – Para resolver este problema, Nuevo México creó en 2022 el Centro de Reforestación de Nuevo México. Este centro está construyendo un invernadero enorme de 14,400 metros cuadrados. Esto aumentará la producción anual de plantas de árboles de unas 250,000 a 5 millones. El proyecto reúne a funcionarios forestales y tres universidades. También investigan cómo cultivar árboles que puedan sobrevivir en un clima más caliente y seco.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo centro ayudará a replantar millones de árboles mucho más rápido, lo cual es vital porque los bosques sostienen las fuentes de agua y previenen inundaciones y erosión. Actualmente, tomaría 50 años replantar solo una gran zona quemada. Al cultivar plantas adaptadas al clima local, los árboles tendrán mejor oportunidad de sobrevivir. El proyecto también une a distintos grupos del suroeste para trabajar juntos en la reforestación.

**Próximo paso** – La construcción del invernadero está por comenzar y el centro empezará a producir muchas más plantas. Los investigadores seguirán probando cómo cultivar árboles resistentes a la sequía y al calor. El centro planea involucrar a comunidades y escuelas para educar sobre la reforestación y apoyar este trabajo a largo plazo.

**Resumen en una frase** – El nuevo Centro de Reforestación de Nuevo México producirá millones de plantas adaptadas al clima para restaurar bosques más rápido y proteger fuentes de agua tras incendios.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a large, vibrant greenhouse filled with rows of young pine seedlings glowing softly under natural light, set against a backdrop of a recovering charred forest and distant mountains, with a rustic chili roaster gently steaming seeds nearby and a small group of silhouetted children planting seedlings beside a fallen log for shade, all rendered in earthy, muted tones reflecting New Mexico’s landscape.

articles.title: Renewables rising, Part 2: Seeking stability amid volatile fossil fuel markets
articles.summary: As global energy markets remain volatile and the price of oil high due to instability and conflict in the Middle East, more countries are looking at investing in renewables to shield their economies from the shocks caused by fossil fuel reliance, rein in import bills – and meet key climate action targets.
articles.content:
Unlike oil and gas, which expose countries to price spikes and geopolitical risk, renewables offer stable, domestically produced power that can strengthen energy security, cut emissions of harmful greenhouse gases and support long-term growth.

In the second in our series focusing on how renewables are shaping the future of energy security, UN News looks at four countries which are striving to increase the share of greener energy sources.

Why it matters:

As The UN Secretary-General António Guterres said in March: “The fastest path to energy security, economic security, and national security is clear: speed up a just transition away from fossil fuels and toward renewable energy.”

Germany: Accelerating the energy transition

The big picture:

Germany has one of the world’s biggest economies and according to its environment agency “renewable energies have undergone constant growth” as part of the European nation’s long-term shift away from fossil fuels.

Renewables are helping reduce reliance on imported fuels and insulating consumers from global price shocks as well as tackling climate change.

© AGEE-Stat

Energy mix:

Renewables provide 55 per cent of electricity consumption

Wind – and to a lesser extent, solar – dominates.

Biomass (energy generated from organic matter) and hydropower are also part of the mix

Fossil fuels are still used in industry and transport

Key challenge:

Wind power is by far the most important energy source, and with that comes sometimes inconsistent levels of power generation. Ensuring that industrial demand is met and safeguarding grid stability remains a major hurdle. Meanwhile, there is a continued need for fossil fuel backup during the transition to renewables.

Progress toward full transition:

Expansion of offshore wind and solar

Grid modernisation and expansion as well as investing in storage facilities are described as “crucial” to the transition process

India: Scaling renewables for development

The big picture:

India has a growing economy and is the most populous country in the world. It still relies on coal to generate the electricity it needs although solar and wind energy is rapidly expanding.

Renewables are helping to reduce costly fuel imports and to stabilise energy supplies in a dynamic economy.

© World Bank

Energy mix:

Renewables account for around 30% of installed capacity (a measure of maximum potential power output)

Solar is rapidly expanding

Coal remains dominant

Key challenge:

Meeting the rapidly rising demand for energy as the economy expands while reducing coal dependence remains challenging, particularly given concerns about the affordability of renewables and the need to ensure reliable power across a vast country.

Progress toward full transition:

Large solar parks and rooftop expansion

Wind and hybrid systems

Green hydrogen initiatives

Solar-powered livelihood programmes are improving incomes and energy access in rural communities. Read more here.

Bolivia: Shifting from gas to renewables

The big picture:

Bolivia is working to diversify away from natural gas, which dominates its energy system. Hydropower is key to the South American country’s long-term energy stability and economic resilience.

© Practical Action Bolivia

Energy mix:

Renewables provide around 30–35% of electricity generation

Hydropower is the main renewable source

Solar and wind are expanding

Natural gas remains dominant

Key challenge:

Bolivia continues to rely on gas revenues and the infrastructure in place to extract, process and deliver the fuel. This heavy structural dependence and accessing sufficient finance for the large-scale deployment of renewables makes a rapid transition away from fossil fuel sources challenging.

Progress toward full transition:

Solar expansion in rural and high-altitude regions

Wind farm development

National roadmap targeting major renewable growth

Read how Solar-powered irrigation is helping rural communities improve agricultural productivity in Bolivia.

Nigeria: Unlocking solar potential

The big picture:

Nigeria is Africa’s largest economy and most populous country. More than 60 per cent of its 241 million people live in poverty according to UN figures.

Despite the development challenges it faces, it has vast renewable potential even though it remains heavily dependent on fossil fuels. Frequent power shortages and reliance on diesel generators highlight the need for a more resilient, renewable-based system.

IOM/Jorge Galindo

Energy mix:

Renewables account for around 20–25 per cent of electricity generation

Hydropower provides most renewable supply

Solar is growing but underdeveloped

Gas dominates electricity generation

Key challenge:

The West African nation’s aim is to generate 50 per cent of its electricity from renewables by 2030 and to do this it will need to build more infrastructure especially a more reliable grid, spend more money and wean industry from its reliance on gas-fired power plants.

Progress toward full transition:

Expansion of off-grid solar and mini-grids

National electrification programmes

Increasing investment in home solar systems

Read how UN-backed solar mini-grid programmes in Nigeria are providing electricity to rural communities, supporting businesses and improving the quality of life.

The bottom line:

Across very different economies, from industrialized Germany to emerging powers like India and developing nations such as Bolivia and Nigeria, a clear trend is emerging:

Renewables are strengthening energy security

They are reducing dependence on volatile fossil fuel markets

They are delivering tangible benefits to people and economies

But challenges remain — from financing and infrastructure to balancing reliability with rapid expansion.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on multiple countries making significant progress in adopting renewable energy to enhance energy security, reduce fossil fuel dependence, and meet climate goals. It highlights tangible benefits for large populations and economies, providing detailed context on challenges and advancements, thus representing a meaningful positive impact on society and the environment.
articles.personality_title: Countries boost renewable energy to reduce fossil fuel risks
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many countries face unstable fossil fuel prices and supply problems, especially due to conflicts in the Middle East. This uncertainty makes it hard for economies to grow steadily and keeps energy costs high.

**What happened** – Four countries—Germany, India, Bolivia, and Nigeria—are increasing their use of renewable energy like wind, solar, and hydropower. They aim to lower their reliance on oil, gas, and coal, which are expensive and affected by global events. Each country is at a different stage but making steady progress, such as Germany using wind power for over half its electricity and India rapidly building solar parks.

**Impact** – Switching to renewables helps these countries protect themselves from sudden price changes and supply disruptions. It also supports cleaner air and fights climate change. For example, Nigeria is working to provide electricity to rural areas with solar mini-grids, improving lives and local businesses. These efforts show how renewable energy can support both economic stability and environmental goals.

**What's next step** – The countries plan to expand renewable projects, improve power grids, and find funding to speed up the transition. Challenges remain, like making sure power is reliable and affordable while building new infrastructure. Continued investment and technology improvements will be key to reaching their energy goals.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Germany, India, Bolivia, and Nigeria are advancing renewable energy to secure stable power, reduce fossil fuel dependence, and support their economies amid global energy uncertainties.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des pays renforcent les énergies renouvelables pour réduire les risques des énergies fossiles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux pays font face à des prix instables et à des problèmes d’approvisionnement en énergies fossiles, notamment à cause des conflits au Moyen-Orient. Cette incertitude complique la croissance économique et maintient les coûts énergétiques élevés.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Quatre pays — Allemagne, Inde, Bolivie et Nigeria — augmentent leur utilisation d’énergies renouvelables comme l’éolien, le solaire et l’hydroélectricité. Ils cherchent à réduire leur dépendance au pétrole, gaz et charbon, coûteux et sensibles aux événements mondiaux. Chaque pays progresse à son rythme, par exemple l’Allemagne utilise l’éolien pour plus de la moitié de son électricité et l’Inde construit rapidement des centrales solaires.

**Impact** – Passer aux renouvelables aide ces pays à se protéger des variations soudaines des prix et des ruptures d’approvisionnement. Cela contribue aussi à un air plus propre et à la lutte contre le changement climatique. Par exemple, le Nigeria développe des mini-réseaux solaires pour électrifier les zones rurales, améliorant la vie et les activités locales. Ces efforts montrent que les renouvelables soutiennent à la fois stabilité économique et environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les pays prévoient d’étendre les projets renouvelables, d’améliorer les réseaux électriques et de trouver des financements pour accélérer la transition. Des défis restent, comme garantir un approvisionnement fiable et abordable tout en construisant de nouvelles infrastructures. Les investissements et progrès technologiques seront essentiels pour atteindre leurs objectifs énergétiques.

**Une phrase clé** – L’Allemagne, l’Inde, la Bolivie et le Nigeria développent les énergies renouvelables pour assurer un approvisionnement stable, diminuer la dépendance aux énergies fossiles et soutenir leurs économies face aux incertitudes mondiales.
articles.personality_title_es: Países impulsan energías renovables para reducir riesgos de combustibles fósiles
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos países enfrentan precios inestables y problemas de suministro de combustibles fósiles, especialmente debido a conflictos en Medio Oriente. Esta incertidumbre dificulta el crecimiento económico y mantiene altos los costos de energía.

**Qué pasó** – Cuatro países—Alemania, India, Bolivia y Nigeria—están aumentando el uso de energías renovables como la eólica, solar e hidroeléctrica. Buscan reducir su dependencia del petróleo, gas y carbón, que son caros y afectados por eventos globales. Cada país avanza a su ritmo, por ejemplo Alemania usa la energía eólica para más de la mitad de su electricidad e India construye rápidamente parques solares.

**Impacto** – Cambiar a renovables ayuda a estos países a protegerse de cambios bruscos en precios y cortes de suministro. También apoya un aire más limpio y combate el cambio climático. Por ejemplo, Nigeria está desarrollando mini-redes solares para llevar electricidad a zonas rurales, mejorando vidas y negocios locales. Estos esfuerzos muestran cómo las renovables pueden apoyar la estabilidad económica y ambiental.

**Próximo paso** – Los países planean ampliar proyectos renovables, mejorar redes eléctricas y conseguir financiamiento para acelerar la transición. Quedan desafíos, como asegurar que la energía sea confiable y asequible mientras construyen nuevas infraestructuras. La inversión continua y mejoras tecnológicas serán clave para alcanzar sus metas energéticas.

**Mensaje en una frase** – Alemania, India, Bolivia y Nigeria avanzan en energías renovables para asegurar energía estable, reducir la dependencia de combustibles fósiles y fortalecer sus economías ante la incertidumbre global.
articles.image_prompt: A harmonious landscape featuring stylized wind turbines, solar panels, and flowing rivers powering vibrant cities and rural areas across diverse terrains representing Germany, India, Bolivia, and Nigeria, with interconnected glowing energy lines symbolizing stable, clean, and locally sourced renewable power strengthening energy security and economic growth.

articles.title: Mezcal worm in a bottle DNA test reveals a surprise
articles.summary: The famous mezcal “worm” has long puzzled scientists, but DNA testing has finally cracked the case. Researchers found that all sampled larvae were actually agave redworm moth caterpillars—not a mix of species as once believed. While the discovery clears up a long-standing mystery, it also raises concerns about sustainability. Growing demand for mezcal and edible larvae could put pressure on wild populations and the agave plants they depend on.
articles.content:
At the bottom of some mezcal bottles sits one of the most recognizable curiosities in the world of spirits: a pale, curled "worm" preserved in alcohol. It has helped give mezcal an air of mystery for decades, but scientists have now shown that this famous bottle stowaway is not a worm at all.

Mezcal is a distilled drink made from agave, the same plant group used to produce tequila. Most bottles are sold without anything added, but a small number contain larvae known as gusanos de maguey (Spanish for agave worms). The tradition feels ancient, but it is actually much more recent than mezcal itself. While mezcal production reaches back centuries in Mexico, the practice of placing larvae in bottles appears to have begun in the 1940s.

A Longstanding Mezcal Mystery

For years, the true identity of these larvae remained uncertain. They had been described as moth larvae, butterfly larvae, and even weevil larvae. Some people suspected more than one species might be involved, especially because the bottled "worms" can vary in color and appearance.

"It's relatively easy to broadly determine the kind of larva based on the shape of the head, but their identity has never been confirmed," said Akito Kawahara, curator at the Florida Museum's McGuire Center for Lepidoptera and Biodiversity. "This is probably because most biologists are not looking inside mezcal bottles."

To settle the question, Kawahara and his colleagues studied mezcal gusanos in research published in 2023 in PeerJ Life & Environment. In 2022, the team traveled to Oaxaca, Mexico, a region deeply tied to mezcal production. They visited distilleries and gathered as many different brands as they could find so they could sample larvae from a variety of bottles.

The larvae did not offer many obvious clues. After sitting in alcohol, their bodies were preserved, but many visible traits that might help identify them were limited. That preservation, however, also protected something far more useful: DNA.

DNA Revealed a Surprising Answer

The researchers were able to extract and analyze genetic material from 18 specimens. They expected the results might point to several different insects, since gusanos de maguey are harvested from the wild rather than raised through a standardized commercial system.

One leading suspect was the tequila giant skipper (Aegiale hesperiaris), a butterfly whose caterpillars feed on agave plants. Its large, whitish larvae seemed like a strong match for many of the pale gusanos seen in mezcal bottles. Its name also made it an obvious candidate.

But the DNA told a different story. Every larva that produced usable genetic data matched the agave redworm moth (Comadia redtenbacheri). In the PeerJ study, specimens that did not produce usable DNA were also identified morphologically as the same species.

That finding suggests the mezcal "worm" is not a mix of random agave insects. At least in the sampled bottles, it was consistently the caterpillar of a single moth species. The researchers also proposed an explanation for the pale "white worm" appearance reported in some bottles: larvae that spend long periods in alcohol may lose some of their reddish color over time.

Why This Tiny Larva Matters

The discovery arrives as mezcal continues to grow far beyond its traditional markets. Its popularity has surged internationally, helped by consumer interest in artisanal spirits and small batch production.

That growth raises difficult questions. Tequila is often produced at industrial scale, but mezcal is still commonly made in smaller facilities across Mexico's dry countryside. Producers roast the rounded hearts of agave plants in fire pits or kilns, then crush and ferment the cooked material before distilling it in smaller batches. As demand rises, it remains unclear whether all producers, landowners, and agave ecosystems can scale up without causing long term harm.

The same concern applies to the agave redworm moth. Its larvae, also known as chinicuiles, have been eaten in Mexico for centuries and are an important part of traditional cuisine. But wild harvesting can be intense, and the insects are not simply picked from the surface of a plant. Red agave caterpillars tunnel into the core of their host agaves, and collecting them often kills the plant.

"Agave worms are still fairly common, but the impact of mezcal becoming popular can have long-term negative effects on local populations because they are harvested in the wild," Kawahara said.

Newer Research Adds a Warning

More recent research has sharpened the sustainability concern. A 2025 study in Botanical Sciences examined chinicuil extraction from Agave applanata populations and found that populations without extraction had higher growth rates. The study reported that larvae extraction could reduce agave populations by up to 57 percent, with juvenile plants especially affected because they are often harvested for larvae even though they are important for population persistence.

That work focused on agave populations rather than mezcal bottles, but it reinforces the same broader issue: the market for edible agave larvae can affect both the insect and the plant it depends on. The study also noted that harvesting can require sacrificing the agave before it reaches sexual maturity, which can alter future population dynamics.

For mezcal producers and harvesters, that could make sustainable production more complicated. If demand for bottles containing gusanos continues to rise, local communities may need better ways to manage wild harvesting, raise larvae on agave farms, or develop methods for producing them without destroying host plants.

The mezcal worm may have started as a marketing novelty, but DNA has turned it into something more interesting: a small creature with a clear identity, a deep connection to agave landscapes, and a future tied to how carefully mezcal's growing popularity is managed.
articles.reasoning: The article reveals a scientific breakthrough in identifying the mezcal 'worm' through DNA testing, clarifying a longstanding mystery. It highlights the environmental and sustainability concerns tied to the growing demand for mezcal and edible larvae, emphasizing the need for sustainable harvesting practices to protect wild agave and insect populations. This has broad significance for environmental conservation and sustainable agriculture linked to a culturally important product.
articles.personality_title: DNA test reveals the true identity of the mezcal 'worm' and its environmental challenge
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Mezcal is a traditional Mexican drink made from agave plants. Some bottles contain a larva, often called a 'worm,' which has puzzled scientists for many years. People were unsure what kind of insect it really was.

**What happened** – In 2023, researchers used DNA testing to study larvae found in mezcal bottles from Oaxaca, Mexico. They discovered that all the larvae tested were from the same species: the agave redworm moth caterpillar, not a mix of insects as previously thought.

**Impact** – This finding solves a long-standing mystery about the mezcal 'worm.' But it also raises concerns because the larvae are collected from wild agave plants, and harvesting them can harm these plants and reduce insect populations. As mezcal grows more popular worldwide, this could put pressure on the environment.

**What's next step** – To protect agave plants and the moths, producers and communities may need to find better ways to harvest larvae sustainably. This could include farming the larvae or developing new methods to avoid damaging wild plants.

**One-sentence takeaway** – DNA tests have identified the mezcal 'worm' as a single moth species, highlighting the need for careful harvesting to protect both the insect and agave plants as mezcal's popularity grows.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un test ADN révèle la vraie identité du « ver » de mezcal et un défi environnemental
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le mezcal est une boisson traditionnelle mexicaine fabriquée à partir de plantes d’agave. Certaines bouteilles contiennent une larve, souvent appelée « ver », qui a longtemps intrigué les scientifiques. On ne savait pas de quel insecte il s’agissait vraiment.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2023, des chercheurs ont utilisé un test ADN pour étudier les larves trouvées dans des bouteilles de mezcal d’Oaxaca, au Mexique. Ils ont découvert que toutes les larves testées appartenaient à la même espèce : la chenille du papillon de nuit agave redworm, et non un mélange d’insectes comme on le pensait.

**Impact** – Cette découverte résout un mystère ancien sur le « ver » du mezcal. Mais elle soulève aussi des inquiétudes, car les larves sont récoltées dans la nature sur les plantes d’agave, et cette récolte peut endommager les plantes et réduire les populations d’insectes. Avec la popularité grandissante du mezcal dans le monde, cela pourrait peser sur l’environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Pour protéger les plantes d’agave et les papillons, les producteurs et les communautés devront trouver des méthodes de récolte plus durables. Cela pourrait passer par l’élevage des larves ou le développement de nouvelles techniques pour éviter d’abîmer les plantes sauvages.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les tests ADN ont identifié le « ver » du mezcal comme une seule espèce de papillon, soulignant la nécessité d’une récolte responsable pour protéger l’insecte et les agaves face à la montée en popularité du mezcal.
articles.personality_title_es: Prueba de ADN revela la verdadera identidad del 'gusano' del mezcal y un reto ambiental
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El mezcal es una bebida tradicional mexicana hecha de plantas de agave. Algunas botellas contienen una larva, llamada comúnmente 'gusano', que ha desconcertado a los científicos durante años. No se sabía exactamente qué insecto era.

**Qué pasó** – En 2023, investigadores usaron pruebas de ADN para estudiar larvas en botellas de mezcal de Oaxaca, México. Descubrieron que todas las larvas eran de la misma especie: la oruga de la polilla agave redworm, y no una mezcla de insectos como se creía.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo resuelve un misterio antiguo sobre el 'gusano' del mezcal. Pero también genera preocupación porque las larvas se recolectan de plantas de agave silvestres, y esta práctica puede dañar las plantas y reducir las poblaciones de insectos. Con la creciente popularidad del mezcal, esto podría afectar al medio ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – Para proteger las plantas de agave y las polillas, los productores y comunidades deberán encontrar formas de recolectar las larvas de manera sostenible, como criarlas en granjas o crear métodos que no dañen las plantas silvestres.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las pruebas de ADN identificaron al 'gusano' del mezcal como una sola especie de polilla, mostrando la necesidad de una recolección cuidadosa para proteger al insecto y las plantas de agave ante la creciente demanda del mezcal.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a clear glass mezcal bottle resting gently on a rustic wooden table, with a softly glowing, pale curled agave redworm moth larva suspended inside the bottle’s bottom, surrounded by stylized agave plants with subtle red and green hues in the background, all rendered in natural, simple colors with a harmonious, inviting atmosphere.

articles.title: Boats to rock pools: Marine makeover for yacht club seawall
articles.summary: Ten artificial rock pools have been installed along Strangford Yacht Club's seawall as a refuge for marine life.
articles.content: "We hope to see a range of different marine life, stuff that you would normally find in your inter-tidal so your crabs, your starfish, your sea snails, loads of different types of seaweed but we are also hoping to see stuff we are not expecting as well," she said.
articles.reasoning: The installation of artificial rock pools along a yacht club seawall creates new habitats for marine life, promoting biodiversity and conservation with tangible environmental benefits that can inspire broader ecological restoration efforts.
articles.personality_title: Artificial rock pools bring new life to yacht club seawall
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Strangford Yacht Club has a seawall that meets the sea, but like many man-made structures, it doesn’t naturally support much marine life. Rock pools, which are small natural pools of seawater found along rocky shores, usually host many sea creatures and plants.

**What happened** – Recently, ten artificial rock pools were built along the seawall at Strangford Yacht Club. These pools are designed to act like real rock pools, providing a safe home for sea animals like crabs, starfish, and sea snails, as well as different types of seaweed.

**Impact** – This project creates new places for marine life to live where there were few before. It helps increase the variety of sea creatures in the area, which is important for a healthy ocean environment. It also offers a chance to see unexpected species, showing how nature can adapt and grow when given the right conditions.

**What's next step** – Scientists and club members will watch the pools over time to see which animals and plants settle there. This information can help guide similar projects in other places, encouraging more efforts to restore ocean life along man-made structures.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Adding artificial rock pools to a seawall has created new homes for marine creatures and could inspire more ocean-friendly designs worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des bassins artificiels redonnent vie au mur de quai du club nautique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le club nautique de Strangford possède un mur de quai en bord de mer qui, comme beaucoup de structures construites par l’homme, ne favorise pas naturellement la vie marine. Les bassins rocheux, petites piscines naturelles d’eau de mer sur les côtes rocheuses, abritent habituellement de nombreux animaux et plantes marins.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Récemment, dix bassins artificiels ont été installés le long du mur de quai du club nautique de Strangford. Ces bassins sont conçus pour imiter les bassins rocheux naturels et offrir un refuge sûr aux animaux marins comme les crabes, les étoiles de mer, les escargots de mer, ainsi qu’à différentes algues.

**Impact** – Ce projet crée de nouveaux habitats pour la vie marine là où il y en avait peu auparavant. Il aide à augmenter la diversité des créatures marines dans la zone, ce qui est important pour un environnement océanique sain. Il offre aussi la possibilité de voir des espèces inattendues, montrant comment la nature peut s’adapter et se développer lorsqu’elle reçoit les bonnes conditions.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques et les membres du club surveilleront les bassins avec le temps pour voir quels animaux et plantes s’y installent. Ces informations pourront guider des projets similaires ailleurs, encourageant plus d’efforts pour restaurer la vie marine le long des structures construites par l’homme.

**Une phrase clé** – L’ajout de bassins artificiels à un mur de quai a créé de nouveaux habitats pour les créatures marines et pourrait inspirer des conceptions plus respectueuses des océans dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: Piscinas artificiales traen nueva vida al muro del club náutico
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El club náutico de Strangford tiene un muro que se encuentra con el mar, pero como muchas estructuras hechas por humanos, no sostiene mucha vida marina naturalmente. Las piscinas rocosas, que son pequeñas piscinas naturales de agua de mar en costas rocosas, suelen albergar muchos animales y plantas marinas.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, se instalaron diez piscinas rocosas artificiales a lo largo del muro del club náutico de Strangford. Estas piscinas están diseñadas para actuar como las naturales, proporcionando un hogar seguro para animales marinos como cangrejos, estrellas de mar y caracoles de mar, así como diferentes tipos de algas.

**Impacto** – Este proyecto crea nuevos lugares para que la vida marina viva donde antes había pocos. Ayuda a aumentar la variedad de criaturas marinas en el área, lo cual es importante para un ambiente oceánico saludable. También ofrece la oportunidad de ver especies inesperadas, mostrando cómo la naturaleza puede adaptarse y crecer cuando recibe las condiciones adecuadas.

**Próximo paso** – Científicos y miembros del club observarán las piscinas con el tiempo para ver qué animales y plantas se establecen allí. Esta información puede ayudar a guiar proyectos similares en otros lugares, fomentando más esfuerzos para restaurar la vida oceánica en estructuras hechas por humanos.

**Una frase clave** – Añadir piscinas rocosas artificiales a un muro ha creado nuevos hogares para criaturas marinas y podría inspirar diseños más amigables con el océano en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a calm yacht club seawall transformed into a vibrant intertidal rock pool teeming with colorful crabs, starfish, sea snails, and various types of seaweed, with gentle waves lapping and small boats resting nearby, using soft natural tones to evoke a sense of marine renewal and unexpected underwater life.
